text: Parenchymal transection during hepatectomy is arguably the most important and potentially dangerous step of the operation. Total control of the field during transection is critical to avoid intraoperative hemorrhage as well as postoperative biliary complications. My name is Jason Hawksworth and I'm a transplant and hepatobiliary surgeon. In this video, I'll demonstrate how we perform parenchymal transection for a robotic right hepatectomy.

The surgeon uses ultrasound to mark the middle hepatic vein and the planned transection plane on the liver. The parenchyma is placed under tension and transected using a laparoscopic CUSA and a robotic vessel sealer. Hemlock clips are applied to control larger vascular and biliary pedicles. During the procedure, hepatic vein bleeding occurs and is managed with fibrillar packing and pressure using a Raytech.

The surgeon exposes and divides the caudate process using the laparoscopic CUSA, facilitating the dissection of the right hilar plate and right bile duct. The right hilar plate is encircled with a silk tie and stapled with the robotic stapler. During the parenchymal transection, coils from a preoperative portal vein embolization are encountered. The right hepatic vein is dissected and divided intraparenchymally with the robotic stapler, completing the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon uses the previously placed Raytech as a landmark to guide the transection plane lateral to the inferior vena cava. After completing the transection, Doppler ultrasound confirms patent flow in the left hepatic artery and portal vein within the liver remnant. The transection plane is inspected for hemostasis and biliary leakage, with Surgiflo applied to ensure adequate hemostasis. The liver specimen is placed in a large endobag and extracted through the Pfannenstiel incision.

Again, this is Jason Hawksworth demonstrating some key steps to a successful parenchymal transection during a robotic right hepatectomy. Thanks for watching and be on the lookout for upcoming videos on a variety of hepatobiliary minimally invasive procedures.
